name: learnings-from-pr
description: |
  Pre-PR knowledge capture. Run this skill AFTER the implementation work is
  done but BEFORE `git push` / `gh pr create`. It reads what the wiki already
  says about the area you touched, identifies what your work proves the wiki
  was missing or wrong, and writes ONE small commit directly to
  leadbay/knowledge:main. Most PRs produce zero wiki edits — that's the
  expected outcome for "no surprises" PRs.

  Do NOT invoke this skill: after pushing, after opening a PR, in the
  middle of implementation, or as a substitute for thinking. It's a
  capture step, not a planning step.

  Triggers: "I'm about to push", "before this PR", "/learnings-from-pr",
  the standing instruction in `claude-action`.
version: 1.0.0
allowed-tools:
  - Bash
  - Read
  - Grep
  - Glob
  - Edit
  - Write
---

# /learnings-from-pr — capture genuine new knowledge before pushing

## When to invoke

You have JUST finished implementation. Tests pass. The diff is final. You are
about to run `git push` or `gh pr create`. **STOP.** Run this skill first.

## When NOT to invoke

- After pushing or opening a PR. (Your context shifts to review-reply mode and
  the learnings get diluted.)
- In the middle of implementation. (You don't yet know what was new.)
- When you implemented exactly what the wiki described. (No-op is correct.)
- As a substitute for `/knowledge-explore`. (That skill builds wiki pages from
  live tours; this skill captures incremental deltas from one PR's worth of
  surprises.)

## The discipline that protects the wiki

This skill writes directly to `~/.leadbay/knowledge/wiki/` and pushes to
`leadbay/knowledge:main` — no PR review. The discipline below MUST hold every
time. If you can't follow it, write nothing — `git push` your code and stop.

### Hard caps

- **Max 3 wiki edits per skill invocation.** A typical PR produces 0–1.
- **Max 200 added lines of wiki content total.** If a finding needs more, write
  a question and let `/knowledge-explore` build it next session.
- **Max 1 NEW page per invocation.** Edits to existing pages don't count
  against this.

### Forbidden phrasing in any wiki edit

If your prose contains any of these, do not write — file a question instead:

- "likely" / "probably" / "presumably" / "I think" / "appears to" / "seems to"
- "should" / "would" (when describing how the system behaves; "should" is fine
  in a recipe section telling another agent what to do)
- "to be confirmed" / "TBC" / "needs verification"

### Mandatory evidence per edit

Every wiki delta must cite:

- A file path you touched in this PR (`backend/.../X.kt:NN-MM`), OR
- A captured URL from a live network panel (`POST /1.5/leads/x/y → 204`), OR
- A quoted log/error message you observed (`"foo: bar"`), OR
- A quoted code snippet from the existing codebase (verbatim, with file:line).

If you can't cite, file a question.

## Protocol

### Phase 1: Find the surface area of your change

```bash
# What files did this PR touch?
git diff --name-only $(git merge-base HEAD origin/main)..HEAD

# What top-level areas? (routes/, models/, frontend/screens/, etc.)
```

For each touched area, run `/knowledge-find` (or `lb-knowledge-find` directly)
on the relevant terms. Read the existing wiki coverage. Take notes on what's
already documented.

### Phase 2: Identify the genuine deltas

For each surprise / friction encountered during implementation, classify:

| Surprise type | Wiki target |
|---|---|
| UI label that didn't grep to anything in the backend | row in `wiki/concept/vocabulary.md` AND row in `wiki/pitfall/vocabulary-mismatch.md` |
| Hidden side-effect (e.g. "X also writes Y" or "click on A also fires B") | "Pitfalls" subsection of the relevant existing `wiki/journey/<x>.md` |
| Workflow no journey page covers | NEW `wiki/journey/<workflow>.md` (only if you actually toured it; otherwise file a question) |
| Ambiguity you had to make a judgment call on | `wiki/questions/<topic>.md` |

Anything that doesn't fit one of these → DO NOT WRITE. Push your code. The
wiki is not a diary; it's an action-oriented reference.

### Phase 3: Pre-write checks

Before writing:

```bash
cd ~/.leadbay/knowledge
git pull --rebase --autostash
LB_LANE=cold python3 bin/lb-knowledge-validate-all
```

If validation fails on existing content, abort — don't compound.

### Phase 4: Write

For each delta (max 3):

- **Vocabulary row** — single line in the appropriate table. Format must match the existing rows in [[concept/vocabulary]]. Cite evidence inline.
- **Pitfall subsection on existing journey** — append to the page's `## Pitfalls` section. Lead with the action ("Don't ..." / "Always ..."). One sentence of context. Evidence quote.
- **New journey page** — only if you actually navigated the workflow live during this PR. Use the structure from [[journey/lead-curation]] as a template. Required sections: What the user sees / Captured live / Backend handler quote / Vocabulary trap callout / Adding a new X — recipe / Pitfalls.
- **Question** — `wiki/questions/<date>-<topic>.md`. Include: what's unclear, where you encountered it (PR URL or branch), what you tried, what assumption you ended up making, who could resolve.

### Phase 5: Validate + commit + push

```bash
cd ~/.leadbay/knowledge
LB_LANE=cold python3 bin/lb-knowledge-validate-all     # MUST be OK
git add -A
git diff --cached --stat                              # show me the delta size
git commit -m "learnings: <one-line summary>

From PR ${LB_PR_REPO:-unknown} run ${LB_PR_RUN_URL:-unknown}.

<one paragraph: which surprise(s), what got captured, why this is
worth wiki space>
"
git push origin main
```

### Phase 6: Verify silence is success

After push, **DO NOT** announce in the original PR description that you "also
updated the wiki". The wiki commit is its own audit trail in
`leadbay/knowledge`. Polluting the PR description with wiki noise erodes the
"this is reference, not diary" discipline.

## Self-checks before exiting the skill

- [ ] Did I read the existing wiki for this area? (Or did I just dump?)
- [ ] Each edit has a concrete file path, URL, or quoted text as evidence?
- [ ] Zero forbidden phrases in the prose I wrote?
- [ ] ≤ 3 edits, ≤ 200 added lines, ≤ 1 new page?
- [ ] LB_LANE=cold validator passes?
- [ ] If unsure about ANY of the above, did I write a question instead of a wiki claim?

## What "no-op" looks like (the most common outcome)

```
$ /learnings-from-pr

Read existing wiki for the area: 4 pages found.
Touched files: routes/LeadsRoutes.kt, frontend/screens/dashboard/...
Surprises encountered: 0 (everything followed the lead-curation journey
                          page's recipe; no new findings).
Result: no-op — nothing to capture. Pushing the original PR.
```

That is success. The wiki stays clean.
